title: Denver Crime, 1985–2026 — The Recent Turn, Across 78 Neighborhoods
titleOptions: Denver Crime Fell 20% From Its 2022 Peak — 78 Neighborhoods, Mapped Month by Month
70,227 to 55,942: Denver's Recent Crime Decline, Mapped Across 78 Neighborhoods
description:
Forty years of reported crime in Denver — the long FBI-era swings, then a real, recent decline: down 20% from the 2022 peak, mapped across all 78 neighborhoods.

This is a fully sourced, reproducible data visualization. Every figure comes straight from the FBI Uniform Crime Reporting program and the Denver Police Department's own open incident data. Every dot on the map is a real reported incident location, anonymized to the block by the source. Coverage and caveats are shown on screen.

Denver's open data publishes a rolling five-year window plus year-to-date, and omits sex offenses. The FBI's classic 'index' crimes (through 2020) and DPD's incident data (2021+) are different measures — shown as two chapters, never merged into one line.

▶ CHAPTERS
0:00  Hook — the recent turn
0:08  What you're about to see
0:22  Chapter 1 — the long arc
2:30  The last five years — up close
2:43  Chapter 2 — the map, month by month
4:52  The busiest & safest areas
5:18  Sources & credits

▶ MADE BY AN AI — FULLY TRANSPARENT
This video was produced end-to-end by Claude (Anthropic's AI): it located and fetched the official source data, built and validated the data pipeline, verified every on-screen figure against the sources, designed and rendered the visuals, and generated the music. No human edited the numbers. Every step — code, data, and design decisions — is public and reproducible in the repository linked below. If you find an error, open an issue; corrections are part of the record.

▶ CODE, DATA & FULL SOURCES (reproduce every number)
https://github.com/hadi-nayebi/crime-cartography/tree/main/videos/denver-co

Music generated with Stable Audio Open (Stability AI). Basemap © OpenStreetMap contributors.

If this was worth a look, please Like, Subscribe, and Share.
